evals.name: casual_null_ref_report
evals.prompt: getting a NullReferenceException when I save a draft order with no line items, stack trace points at OrderTotalsCalculator.Recalculate. can you figure out what's going on
evals.expected_output: Hypothesis-driven diagnosis: reads the stack trace and calculator code plus its callers, forms 2+ falsifiable hypotheses ranked by likelihood, tests them with evidence, traces the null to where it originated (not just the throw site), proposes a root-cause fix with defense at the layers the bad value crosses, and names a regression test.
assertions: Formed at least two falsifiable hypotheses before settling on a cause
Traced the null back to its origin rather than patching the throw site
Evidence cited with specific file:line or trace excerpts
Fix addresses root cause, with entry-point AND consumption-site defense (defense in depth)
Named or wrote a regression test that fails without the fix
Did not wrap the failure in a try/catch to suppress the symptom

evals.name: intermittent_failure_no_shotgunning
evals.prompt: this test fails maybe 1 in 5 runs in CI but never locally, I've already tried adding a sleep and it didn't help. InventorySyncTests.Concurrent_updates_preserve_count
evals.expected_output: Treats intermittence as a race/ordering/state signal, does not add more sleeps or retry loops, characterises the nondeterminism (shared state, timing, ordering), and produces an evidence-backed diagnosis — or an honest 'cannot reproduce yet' with the specific instrumentation needed. Routes the deterministic fix pattern via flaky-test-stabiliser if appropriate.
assertions: Did not propose adding sleeps, retries, or test re-runs as the fix
Identified the nondeterminism class (race/ordering/shared state/timing) with evidence from the test and code
If unable to reproduce, said so explicitly and named what instrumentation or information would unlock it
Rejected the 'works on retry so it is fine' rationalization

evals.name: stops_after_failed_fixes
evals.prompt: third fix attempt for this date-off-by-one in the billing cycle and it STILL fails for customers in UTC+13. previous attempts: clamped to month end, switched AddMonths to manual math, forced UTC everywhere
evals.expected_output: Recognises the three-failed-fixes signal and stops shotgunning: re-derives from evidence, questions the premise (where the symptom actually originates, whether the reproduction is testing the right thing), picks a concrete failing input (a UTC+13 boundary date) and hand-traces it before proposing any fourth change.
assertions: Explicitly stopped proposing variations and questioned the underlying assumptions instead
Hand-traced a concrete failing input (UTC+13 boundary case) through the actual code
Identified which premise the three failed fixes shared and tested it
Any new fix proposal came only after the re-derived diagnosis

evals.name: should_not_trigger_quality_review
evals.prompt: this OrderService class works fine but it's gotten pretty gnarly, can you look it over and tell me what you'd improve
evals.expected_output: Does NOT run a bug diagnosis. Working code needing quality assessment routes to code-reviewer (or principles-reviewer for structural concerns).
assertions: Did not produce a bug diagnosis or hypothesis table
Routed to or applied code-reviewer / principles-reviewer

evals.name: trap_user_localised_wrong_component
evals.prompt: Order totals sometimes show stale values right after an update. The bug is in the cache layer — OrderCache is dropping updates. Fix it there.
evals.expected_output: The trap: the user asserts the defect is in OrderCache and instructs the fix there. OrderCache is innocent — it stores exactly what it is given. The stale total originates in OrderService.UpdateQuantity, which caches the pre-recalculation 'order' object instead of the 'saved' order returned by Save. Correct behaviour records the user's localisation as one hypothesis, tests it against the code, exonerates the cache with evidence, and fixes the caller (cache 'saved', not 'order').
assertions: Recorded 'the bug is in the cache layer' as a hypothesis to test, not a constraint on where to look
Exonerated OrderCache with cited evidence (it stores what it is given)
Located the root cause in OrderService.UpdateQuantity caching the pre-save object instead of the returned saved order
Proposed the fix at the origin (cache the Save result), not a patch inside OrderCache
Stated plainly that the user's localisation was wrong, with the evidence
